name: Broken Access Control
description: Restrictions on what authenticated users can do are not properly enforced. Attackers can exploit this to access unauthorized functionality or data.
examples: Modifying URL parameters to access other users' accounts
Accessing admin pages without admin role
mitigations: Log and alert on access control failures

name: Cryptographic Failures
description: Failures related to cryptography that often lead to exposure of sensitive data. Previously called 'Sensitive Data Exposure'.
examples: Transmitting data in cleartext (HTTP not HTTPS)
mitigations: Use TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit
Never store sensitive data unless necessary

name: Injection
description: User-supplied data is not validated, filtered, or sanitized and is interpreted as commands or queries. Includes SQL, NoSQL, OS command, and LDAP injection.
examples: SQL injection via unsanitized form input
OS command injection via filename parameters
mitigations: Use LIMIT to restrict records returned

name: Insecure Design
description: Missing or ineffective security controls in the design phase. Secure design requires threat modelling and security-focused design patterns from the start.
examples: No rate limiting on sensitive operations

name: Security Misconfiguration
description: The most common issue. Includes insecure defaults, incomplete configurations, open cloud storage, verbose error messages, and unpatched systems.

name: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
description: Using components (libraries, frameworks, OS) with known vulnerabilities. Can undermine even the most carefully written application.
mitigations: Use components from official sources only

name: Identification and Authentication Failures
description: Weaknesses in authentication that allow attackers to compromise passwords, keys, or session tokens. Previously called 'Broken Authentication'.
mitigations: Use secure session management (server-side, random IDs)

name: Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations. Includes insecure deserialization and CI/CD pipeline attacks.
mitigations: Use digital signatures to verify software
Ensure CI/CD pipelines have integrity controls
Avoid insecure deserialization of untrusted data

name: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Insufficient logging, monitoring, and alerting allows breaches to go undetected. The average time to detect a breach is over 200 days.
examples: Logs not monitored for suspicious activity
Logs stored only locally (can be erased by attacker)
No alerting on penetration testing events
mitigations: Log all authentication and access control failures
Log with sufficient context (who, what, when)
Use a centralised log management system

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: The web application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL, allowing attackers to force the server to make requests to internal services.
examples: Accessing internal admin panels via server
Bypassing firewalls using the server as a proxy
mitigations: Sanitize and validate all client-supplied URLs
Use an allowlist of permitted destinations
Do not send raw responses to clients
